title: Decimals
blurb: Place value past the dot, comparing, rounding, and the four operations — built on a 10×10 grid you can shade.

lessons.title: Decimal Place Value
lessons.concreteComparison: A price tag of \$2.45 means 2 whole dollars, 4 dimes, and 5 pennies. A dime is one **tenth** of a dollar and a penny is one **hundredth** — so the digits after the dot are counting tenths and hundredths. That dot is a **decimal point**, and $2.45$ is a **decimal**.
lessons.objective: Read and write decimals to the thousandths place, name the value of each digit, and see a decimal as a fraction with a denominator of 10, 100, or 1000.
concept: A **decimal** uses a dot to show parts of a whole smaller than 1. The first digit after the dot is **tenths** (out of 10), the next is **hundredths** (out of 100), the next is **thousandths** (out of 1000).
Each place is **10 times smaller** than the one to its left — the same pattern as ones, tens, hundreds, just running the other way past the dot.
Because the places are tenths, hundredths, and thousandths, every decimal is just a **fraction with a denominator that is a power of 10**. So $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.
lessons.keyIdea: The digits after the decimal point are tenths, hundredths, thousandths — each place 10× smaller than the last. A decimal is a fraction whose denominator is 10, 100, or 1000.

facts.title: Place value after the dot
facts.explanation: Tenths, then hundredths, then thousandths — each ten times smaller.

workedExamples.solution: The last digit (7) is in the hundredths place, so the denominator is 100: $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.

workedExamples.statement: Write $0.6$ as a fraction in simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: One digit after the dot means tenths: $0.6 = \frac{6}{10} = \frac{3}{5}$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading $0.6$ and $0.06$ as the same size.
commonMistakes.correction: $0.6$ is six tenths; $0.06$ is six hundredths — ten times smaller.
Count the digits after the dot: two digits → hundredths → $\frac{37}{100}$.

tryIt.statement: Write $0.08$ as a fraction in simplest form.
tryIt.solution: The 8 is in the hundredths place: $0.08 = \frac{8}{100} = \frac{2}{25}$.

tryIt.statement: Write $0.125$ as a fraction in simplest form.
tryIt.solution: Three digits after the dot means thousandths: $0.125 = \frac{125}{1000} = \frac{1}{8}$.

steps.title: One whole, cut into 100
steps.teach: The big square is **one whole**. Each small square is one **hundredth** ($0.01$), and a full column of ten is one **tenth** ($0.1$). Tap to build a decimal and watch the fraction keep up.

steps.title: Tenths and hundredths
beats: The first digit after the dot counts **tenths** — full columns of the grid.
The second digit counts **hundredths** — single squares.
So $0.37$ is **3 tenths and 7 hundredths**: three full columns plus seven squares.
Together that is $\frac{3}{10} + \frac{7}{100} = \frac{37}{100}$.

steps.title: A decimal is a fraction in disguise
beats: Count the digits after the dot — that tells you the denominator.
One digit → tenths (over 10). Two digits → hundredths (over 100).
$0.6$ is six full columns: $\frac{6}{10}$, which simplifies to $\frac{3}{5}$.
Same amount, two costumes — a decimal and a fraction.

steps.title: Reading a decimal as a fraction
steps.intro: Count the digits after the dot to choose the denominator, then simplify if you can.

steps: The last digit (7) is in the **hundredths** place, so the denominator is 100.
$0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$ — nothing divides 37 and 100, so it is already simplest.

examples.prompt: Write $0.6$ as a fraction in simplest form.
steps: One digit after the dot → **tenths**, so $0.6 = \frac{6}{10}$.

steps.title: Read the grid
steps.intro: Each grid is one whole. Count the columns (tenths) and squares (hundredths).

problems.prompt: Which decimal does this grid show?

problems.explanation: The 8 is in the hundredths place → $\frac{8}{100}$.

problems.prompt: What is $0.05$ as a fraction in simplest form?

steps.title: One more place: thousandths
beats: Add a third digit after the dot and you reach **thousandths** — out of 1000.
$0.125$ means 1 tenth, 2 hundredths, and 5 thousandths.
As a fraction that is $\frac{125}{1000}$, which simplifies to $\frac{1}{8}$.
Every step right past the dot makes the place ten times smaller.

steps.title: Naming the place value
steps.intro: The place tells you the value: 1st = tenths, 2nd = hundredths, 3rd = thousandths.

examples.prompt: In $0.125$, what is the value of the digit **2**?
steps: The 2 is the **second** digit after the dot → hundredths.

examples.prompt: Write $0.125$ as a fraction in simplest form.
steps: Three digits after the dot → **thousandths**, so $\frac{125}{1000}$.

steps.title: Which place, which value?
steps.intro: Read the position of each digit after the dot.

problems.prompt: In $0.349$, which digit is in the **thousandths** place?
problems.explanation: Third place after the dot = thousandths = 9.

problems.prompt: Which is **larger**: the value of 4 in $0.4$, or the value of 4 in $0.04$?
Write $0.2$ as a fraction in simplest form.

steps.title: You already read decimals every day
steps.body: Every price tag is a decimal. \$4.99 is 4 whole dollars and 99 hundredths of a dollar — 99 cents. You have been reading hundredths your whole life.

steps.title: Decimal to fraction
steps.explanation: Two digits after the dot → hundredths → $\frac{37}{100}$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The places after the dot are **tenths, hundredths, thousandths** — each 10× smaller than the last.
A decimal is a **fraction over 10, 100, or 1000**: $0.37 = \frac{37}{100}$.
Count the digits after the dot to pick the denominator, then simplify.

lessons.title: Comparing & Ordering Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: A pen costs \$0.50 and a pencil \$0.45. Which is cheaper? The pencil: $0.45 < 0.50$ — even though 45 looks bigger than 5, because $0.45$ is four-tenths-and-a-bit while $0.50$ is five tenths. Comparing decimals means comparing **place by place**, not counting digits.
lessons.objective: Compare two decimals by lining up the decimal points and checking place value from the left, and put several decimals in order — knowing that extra digits do not make a decimal larger.
concept: To compare decimals, **line up the decimal points** and read from the **left**: tenths first, then hundredths, then thousandths.
The first place where the digits differ decides it. For $0.5$ and $0.45$, the tenths are 5 vs 4, so $0.5 > 0.45$.
Putting a zero on the **end** changes nothing: $0.5 = 0.50$. Writing both with the same number of places makes them easy to line up.
lessons.keyIdea: Compare from the left, one place at a time — the first place that differs wins. More digits does NOT mean a bigger decimal.

facts.title: Trailing zeros are free
facts.explanation: Zeros on the right don't change the value; they just show more places.

commonMistakes.correction: Line up places: tenths 4 vs 5, so $0.45 < 0.5$. Digit count doesn't decide size.
$0.5 = 0.50$ — a trailing zero adds no value.

steps.title: Which one is bigger?
steps.teach: Both grids are one whole. $0.5$ fills five full columns; $0.45$ fills four columns and five squares. Tap the sign that makes it true — the shaded amount never lies.

steps.title: Compare from the left
beats: Line up the decimal points and read **left to right**.
The first place that differs decides it — here the **tenths**: 5 beats 4.
So $0.5 > 0.45$, even though 45 has more digits than 5.
On a number line, $0.45$ sits just to the left of $0.5$.

steps.title: Trailing zeros are free
beats: Adding a zero on the **end** of a decimal doesn't change its value: $0.5 = 0.50$.
That lets you give two decimals the **same number of places** before comparing.
To compare $0.5$ and $0.45$, write $0.50$ vs $0.45$ — now just compare $50$ and $45$ hundredths.

steps.title: Comparing two decimals
steps.intro: Line up the points, then compare from the left until the digits differ.

steps.title: Which is larger?
steps.intro: Compare from the left. A number line can help you picture it.

steps.title: Putting decimals in order
beats: To order several decimals, compare the **tenths** first and group them.
Break ties by the **hundredths**, then the **thousandths**.
$0.25$, $0.3$, $0.4$ → tenths 2, 3, 4 → already in order.
Plot them and the order is the left-to-right order on the line.

steps.title: Ordering decimals
steps.intro: Compare tenths first; break ties with the next place.

steps: $0.06$ has 0 tenths; $0.6$ and $0.66$ both have 6 tenths.
Break the tie by hundredths: 0 vs 6, so $0.6 < 0.66$.

problems.prompt: Which ordering (least to greatest) is correct?

steps.title: Hundredths win medals
steps.body: At the 2008 Olympics, swimmer Michael Phelps won a gold medal by **one hundredth of a second** — $0.01$ s. Comparing decimals out to the hundredths place was the whole difference between gold and silver.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Line up the decimal points and compare **from the left**, place by place.
The first place that differs decides it — more digits doesn't mean bigger.
Trailing zeros don't change a decimal: $0.5 = 0.50$.
